plainMeaning: When you sincerely worship the Jina, your troubles fade, your obstacles are cut away, and your mind grows calm and happy.
recitation: Upasargāḥ kṣayaṁ yānti, chidyante vighna-vallayaḥ | manaḥ prasannatām eti, pūjyamāne jineśvare ||

wordByWord.gloss: afflictions, calamities, hostile troubles (nominative plural — the subject)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit upa- ('near, upon') + √sṛj ('to release, send forth') → upasarga, 'that which is hurled at one'; the same term the hymn uses for what it wards off.
wordByWord.notes: In the book this word is printed sandhi-joined to the next (ઉપસર્ગાઃક્ષયં); split here for glossing.

wordByWord.gloss: destruction, dwindling, dissolution (accusative singular — the goal of 'go to')
wordByWord.etymology: From √kṣi ('to waste away, perish'); kṣaya = 'wearing away, ruin'. Idiom kṣayaṁ √yā = 'to come to an end'.

wordByWord.gloss: they go (to), they arrive at — i.e. 'come to (destruction)'
wordByWord.etymology: 3rd person plural present of √yā ('to go'); with kṣayaṁ forms the standard locution 'go to ruin = are destroyed'.

wordByWord.gloss: are cut, are severed, are torn apart (3rd person plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Passive present of √chid ('to cut, cleave'); the passive -ya- stem makes the obstacles the thing acted upon — 'they get cut'.

wordByWord.gloss: the creepers of obstacles — clinging vines of hindrance (nominative plural compound)
wordByWord.etymology: vighna ('obstacle, impediment') + vallī ('creeper, climbing vine'), nom. pl. vallayaḥ. A tatpuruṣa: obstacles imagined as twining, strangling creepers that must be slashed.
wordByWord.notes: Some recensions read vallarī ('a leafy creeper'); sense unchanged.

wordByWord.gloss: the mind (nominative singular — new subject of the second line)
wordByWord.etymology: From √man ('to think'); manas, the thinking-and-feeling faculty.
wordByWord.notes: Printed sandhi-joined to the next word in the book (મનઃપ્રસન્નતામેતિ).

wordByWord.gloss: serenity, clear-calm gladness, limpid tranquillity (accusative singular)
wordByWord.etymology: prasanna ('clear, tranquil, pleased', past participle of pra-√sad, 'to settle, grow clear' — as muddy water settles) + abstract suffix -tā. So 'the state of being clear and glad'.

wordByWord.gloss: goes to, reaches, attains (3rd person singular)
wordByWord.etymology: 3rd sg. present of √i ('to go'); with prasannatām = 'attains serenity'.
Present passive participle of √pūj ('to honour, worship'), locative singular. With jineśvare it forms a locative absolute: 'the Jina being worshipped' = 'when the Jina is worshipped'.
wordByWord.notes: This locative-absolute clause governs the whole verse — it states the condition under which all three blessings occur.

wordByWord.gloss: the Lord of Jinas, the supreme Jina (locative singular)
wordByWord.etymology: jina ('victor, conqueror of the passions') + īśvara ('lord, sovereign') → jineśvara, 'sovereign among the victors'; locative pairing with pūjyamāne.

literalTranslation: Afflictions go to destruction; the creepers of obstacles are cut through; the mind attains serenity — when the Lord of Jinas is being worshipped.
idiomaticTranslation: When the Lord of Jinas is worshipped, every affliction dwindles to nothing, the clinging vines of obstacles are slashed away, and the mind itself grows clear and serene.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza is built on a single hinge: the locative absolute pūjyamāne jineśvare, 'when the Jina is being worshipped.' Everything else is its threefold fruit, and the poet arranges that fruit as a deliberate ascent. First the gross, outward layer — upasargāḥ kṣayaṁ yānti — bodily and circumstantial calamities simply 'go to ruin.' Then the subtler, structural layer — chidyante vighna-vallayaḥ — where vighna (an inner impediment to one's undertakings, not merely an external blow) is pictured as a vallī, a creeper: obstacles do not stand like walls but twine, spread, and strangle, so they must be cut (chid), not pushed aside. The verb's passive voice is exact — the worshipper does not hack at them; in the presence of true pūjā they 'get cut.' Finally the innermost layer — manaḥ prasannatām eti — the mind reaches prasannatā, a word whose root image (pra-√sad) is silt settling out of water until it runs clear. The movement is outside-in: trouble removed, hindrance severed, and only then the still, glad mind. The placement is pointed too: the preceding verse 43 sealed the supremacy of the Jina-śāsana as the highest auspiciousness; this verse turns that abstract glory into a concrete promise to the one who bows; and verse 45 will widen the circle outward again to bless 'all the world.' One quiet but important note: the Jina here is vītarāga — He neither receives the worship as flattery nor dispenses these results as favours. The benefit is intrinsic to the act of devotion itself.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encapsulates the Jain logic of worship: the Tīrthaṅkara is a passionless, liberated soul who grants no boons, so the calming of affliction and the clearing of the mind are not gifts handed down but transformations worked within the devotee's own bhāva (inner disposition). The true summit named here is manaḥ-prasannatā — mental serenity — which is precisely the equanimity (sāmāyika) that the whole pratikramaṇa labours to cultivate; relief from upasarga and vighna is real but instrumental, the outer clearing that lets the inner clarity arise. The progression body → karmic-obstacle → mind mirrors the soul's movement from external entanglement toward the settled, lucid state proper to right conduct.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as a sealing benediction, this verse lets the practitioner reframe worship: one bows to the Jina not to bargain away misfortune but to let the mind settle clear. A reciter facing fear, illness, or a tangle of obstacles can dwell on the creeper-image — obstacles that grow and cling are cut by steady devotion — and take the final quarter, manaḥ prasannatām eti, as the real measure of a successful pūjā: not changed circumstances, but a quieted mind.

crossReferences.text: Uvasaggaharaṁ Pāsaṁ… (the Upasargahara Stotra, Smaraṇa #4) — the same upasarga-removal theme to which this hymn's phala-stuti (v.38) appeals
Sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ… (preceding v.43) and Śivamastu sarva-jagataḥ… (following v.45) — the maṅgala quartet this verse completes
crossReferences.source: appended Sanskrit maṅgala stanzas, Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.

crossReferences.text: This śloka also circulates as a stand-alone maṅgala verse appended to the Bhaktāmara recitation tradition

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)
sources.consultedFor: Root/sense verification for upasarga (upa+√sṛj), kṣaya (√kṣi), √chid, vallī ('creeper'), prasanna/prasannatā (pra-√sad), and √pūj.

sources.title: Jain stotra anthologies / Ajitaśānti Stava recensions (Jain eLibrary)
sources.consultedFor: Placement of the closing Sanskrit maṅgala stanzas (vv.43–46) within the recited Ajitaśānti tradition and their distinct authorship.

sources.title: Uvasaggaharaṁ Stotra (Jain Treasures)
sources.consultedFor: The Jain upasarga-removal (protective recitation) theme that this verse restates as the fruit of worship.

notes: This is the second of the four appended Sanskrit maṅgala ślokas (vv.43–46); it is a widely circulated benediction whose individual authorship differs from Nandiṣeṇasūri (as the preface notes) and is not asserted here. The book prints two visarga-sandhi pairs as single orthographic tokens — ઉપસર્ગાઃક્ષયં (upasargāḥ + kṣayaṁ) and મનઃપ્રસન્નતામેતિ (manaḥ + prasannatām + eti); these are split for glossing but preserved verbatim in the token field. vighna-vallayaḥ is from vallī ('creeper'), nom. pl.; some recensions read vallarī, with no change of sense. pūjyamāne jineśvare is a locative absolute governing all three result-clauses.
